questions.specification: What is the role of secondary research in the design process?

options.content: To validate and support primary findings

questions.specification:
Which stage of the design process is likely being shown in the image?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/15b46111-b630-4e66-9f82-6034b947ab94.jpeg)

options.markscheme: Correct. Ideation involves the generation of various design ideas, typically represented through sketching and brainstorming. Modelling involves creating physical or digital versions of these ideas (such as the mock-up shown) to explore and communicate concepts before final production.

Incorrect. Branding involves developing a brand identity, such as logos and marketing strategies, while pricing involves financial analysis and determining market value. Neither is depicted by the creation of design sketches and physical mock-ups.

Incorrect. Stakeholder outreach is the process of gathering feedback or requirements from people interested in the project. While models can be used during this process, the primary activities shown (sketching and mock-up construction) are internal design development tasks.

Incorrect. Final assembly is a manufacturing stage where the finished components of a product are put together. The sketches and low-fidelity mock-up indicate an early design stage, not the final production of a finished item.

questions.specification:
Topic 1.2 Design thinking and strategies

What aspect of the design thinking process is best represented by the diagram shown below?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/c1055a22-39dd-4a4c-8bfa-51cec9ecc44c.png)

options.content: Identifying and framing the user's problem clearly
options.markscheme: This diagram, known as the 'Design Squiggle', represents the design process. The transition from a chaotic, tangled mess to a single straight line symbolizes the journey from the 'fuzzy front end' (uncertainty, research, and ideation) to a clear, focused definition of the problem and its solution.

options.content: Selecting a final manufacturing technique for mass production
options.markscheme: Selecting manufacturing techniques occurs once the design is already clearly defined and finalized. While represented by the straight line at the end, it does not capture the essence of the entire process shown in the squiggle.

options.content: Conducting materials testing for regulatory compliance
options.markscheme: Materials testing is a specific technical verification step. The squiggle represents the conceptual and creative process of finding focus, rather than technical validation of materials.

options.content: Calculating the total lifecycle cost of the product
options.markscheme: Lifecycle cost analysis is a quantitative evaluation tool. It is not represented by the visual metaphor of the design squiggle, which focuses on the reduction of ambiguity and the development of design clarity.

questions.specification:
What is the role of a persona in design?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXdQ1pekv_iVCcXKbMymEJEMa23oAlTNeUXFWexwbILwFYX-AAo-8iKUq3uPLJm2hlJejNSMe0AmVi0iuDwsO8AwCaSbl2VSV1YhrN6cRFX1JuDx1rgmH-fIbZPbnJxvjCCRIV6c?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: To illustrate the emotions of the designer
To represent end-user characteristics and needs

questions.specification:
What type of activity is taking place in the image?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXcyX2p1ZiOzRxuy8zmHPDy8Sinj9gFunDdS1tXJ9lPTzC8RtgTmuseqtZgtwmDPE92RTBBFOsFS_Xo2StFO_iefp7KWp8nQYWFQkCBC5Lgr1dRpiO4ET3Oq8HtFvRygi79R_hT3NA?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: Product analysis to assess function and design

questions.specification: A design team has been tasked with developing a compact countertop composting appliance that encourages sustainable food waste disposal in small urban kitchens. They use a structured design process to create a solution that is functional, hygienic, and intuitive to use.

parts.content:
The team begins the project by identifying key user needs through stakeholder interviews with eco-conscious urban dwellers.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ea7dc0e8-eb95-4138-991a-5dedbc836fa7)

Identify one user need that might influence the design of a composting appliance.
parts.markscheme:
The appliance should eliminate unpleasant odours in small kitchens. <marks>1 mark</marks>


*Award [1] for any reasonable and relevant user need (e.g., odour control, space saving, ease of cleaning).*

parts.content: Explain two reasons why engaging stakeholders early in the design process is important.
parts.markscheme:
Ensures the final product addresses real user problems. <marks>1 mark</marks>


Builds empathy and increases the chances of adoption upon release. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per justified reason related to user-centred design.*

parts.content:
The designers move into the ideation phase and develop several early concept sketches based on the stakeholder findings.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/097dc9c7-5c7a-4fac-915a-81bda342619b)

Describe three benefits of using sketches during the concept development phase.
parts.markscheme:
Allows quick visualisation of multiple design ideas. <marks>1 mark</marks>


Encourages creativity and brainstorming within the team. <marks>1 mark</marks>


Provides a low-cost tool for receiving early feedback. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per distinct benefit of sketching during ideation.*

parts.content: Outline four features the team should evaluate when selecting the most appropriate concept.
parts.markscheme:
Ease of use for consumers <marks>1 mark</marks>


Manufacturability of the design <marks>1 mark</marks>


Aesthetic appeal for modern kitchens <marks>1 mark</marks>


Environmental sustainability of materials <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per clearly justified feature relevant to product selection.*

parts.content:
After selecting a preferred concept, the team builds a prototype and tests it with a group of users in a simulated environment.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/f723691f-d7fe-4b43-9ee9-5a1a340ed569)

Discuss how user testing with prototypes can influence the final design of the appliance.
parts.markscheme:
Reveals usability issues such as awkward lid operation. <marks>1 mark</marks>


Helps refine ergonomics and comfort in small kitchens. <marks>1 mark</marks>


Identifies unexpected behaviours, like overfilling or incorrect usage. <marks>1 mark</marks>


Encourages changes based on emotional response (e.g., users found it gross to clean). <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per well-justified way that testing leads to design iteration, up to [4 max].*

parts.content: Evaluate the effectiveness of following a structured design process (such as the double diamond) in the development of this composting appliance.
parts.markscheme:
Provides a clear structure to move from problem to solution. <marks>1 mark</marks>


Encourages divergent and convergent thinking through distinct phases. <marks>1 mark</marks>


Facilitates collaboration by aligning the team on objectives. <marks>1 mark</marks>


May slow down innovation if followed too rigidly. <marks>1 mark</marks>


Allows iterative testing and refinement, improving end product quality. <marks>1 mark</marks>


Requires time and resources that may not be feasible for small startups. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per balanced point on advantages or drawbacks, up to [6 max].*

questions.specification: What happens when designers use an iterative model of testing?

options.content: All solutions are tested without revisions
The design process ends after one prototype
Solutions are tested and refined to meet all user needs

parts.content: Identify two types of research used during the design process and briefly define each.
parts.markscheme:
- Primary research: involves collecting first-hand data such as interviews or observations <marks>A1</marks>
- Secondary research: uses existing data from sources like websites, articles, or government publications <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award [1] for each accurate identification and correct definition of the research type, up to [2 max].</note>

questions.specification: Which of the following activities is **LEAST** associated with the modelling stage?
What is the first step in solving a design problem?
What does defining clear design specifications help designers achieve?

parts.content: Evaluate how presenting a solution effectively can influence product success.
parts.markscheme:
- Clear visuals help stakeholders understand the concept. <marks>1</marks>
- Demonstrating key usability features improves user engagement. <marks>1</marks>
- Explaining how the solution meets user needs builds confidence. <marks>1</marks>
- Can differentiate the product from competitors. <marks>1</marks>
- Clarifies how design intentions are met. <marks>1</marks>
- Makes a compelling case for funding or manufacturing. <marks>1</marks>
- Informs marketing and communication strategies. <marks>1</marks>
- Shows alignment with initial research and specifications. <marks>1</marks>
- Engages users in the design story, increasing product acceptance. <marks>1</marks>

*Award [1] for each well-developed point that links effective presentation with success, up to [9 max].*

[9 marks]

questions.specification: The use of personas and storyboards in the early stages of the design process.

parts.content: Discuss how designers use personas and storyboards in the early stages of design.
parts.markscheme:
- Personas represent user groups’ needs, motivations, and challenges <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They are based on research data and help guide user-centred decisions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Storyboards map the user’s experience step-by-step <marks>1 mark</marks>
- These tools help identify pain points and opportunities for innovation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Storyboards assist in visualising interactions in context <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Both tools promote empathy and inclusivity in design <marks>1 mark</marks>


*Award [1] for each valid and clearly developed explanation, up to [6 max].*

questions.specification: What is the final aim of product analysis in the design process?
What needs to be clearly communicated for a new product to be manufactured?
Why do designers use prototypes and mock-ups?

parts.content: Explain two ways product analysis supports the design process.
parts.markscheme:
- It helps designers understand how existing products solve similar problems. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It reveals key performance features and usability issues for inspiration or improvement. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Product analysis helps define functional requirements for a new solution. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It supports material selection based on proven effectiveness. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Award $2$ marks for each well-explained way ($1$ mark for identifying the way and $1$ mark for the explanation), up to a maximum of $4$ marks. <marks>0 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Designers often apply user-centred design (UCD) approaches when developing everyday consumer products to meet specific user needs and improve usability.

parts.content:
The image shows a range of kitchen vegetable peelers with varying handle shapes and grip textures.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/6740ed75-0ce3-4057-9bf1-2fb5b3ca5ac6)

Define the term “user-centred design” in the context of product development.
parts.markscheme:
- A design approach that focuses on the needs, preferences, and limitations of end-users at every stage of the design process. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for a concise, accurate definition relevant to product development.*

parts.content: State one user research method that could have been used to test the effectiveness of the different peeler handles.
parts.markscheme:
- User observation. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for identifying any valid method such as interviews, focus groups, user observation, or surveys.*

parts.content:
The image shows an elderly person attempting to use a microwave interface with many small icons.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/c8296de0-0bbb-4b1b-be3c-903b56ac6d87)

Explain how this product interface could fail to meet the needs of a specific user persona.
parts.markscheme:
- The small icons may be difficult for older users to read due to deteriorating eyesight. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Complex touch interfaces can confuse users unfamiliar with digital interfaces. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per valid explanation related to physical or cognitive limitations of the user persona.*

parts.content: Describe two ways a UCD approach could improve the usability of this microwave interface. [2]
parts.markscheme:
- Use of larger, high-contrast buttons for better visibility. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Simplifying options and adding voice guidance to reduce cognitive load. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per clear, feasible suggestion aligned with UCD principles.*

parts.content:
The image shows two versions of a public transport ticket machine—one with text-only labels and one with symbols and color-coded categories.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/bd60cbb0-0167-4cc6-b428-634f6f197690)

Evaluate how applying usability objectives could guide improvements in this interface.
parts.markscheme:
- Learnability: Users will quickly learn the system with symbols and consistent color cues. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Efficiency: Streamlined categorization helps users complete transactions faster. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Errors: Clear navigation reduces mis-clicks or incorrect ticket selections. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Satisfaction: A smoother experience leads to less frustration and positive user feedback. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for each relevant usability objective applied meaningfully to the design context.*

parts.content: Discuss how user-centred design can contribute to more inclusive public product experiences.
parts.markscheme:
- Involves diverse user groups in the design process. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Addresses needs of people with disabilities, elderly, or low literacy levels. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps remove barriers by using intuitive layouts and universal design principles. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Promotes empathy through task analysis and persona development. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Leads to higher adoption and satisfaction rates across user demographics. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Builds brand trust and meets legal accessibility requirements. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per well-explained point up to [6] marks, including reference to inclusion, empathy, and UCD principles.*

questions.specification: What is the benefit of iterative evaluation of design ideas?
What is essential when presenting a solution using a storyboard?

options.content: Showing how the solution solves the problem

questions.specification:
Why is the activity in the image valuable in the design process?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/a8303b49-9aa3-4982-aa7f-337cace8d26f.jpeg)

options.content: To gather direct insights into user needs and problems

questions.specification: Why are user-centred methods crucial at the start of the design process?

A startup is developing a smart helmet for cyclists that includes turn signals, crash detection, and voice navigation. The product is aimed at urban commuters and must undergo a full design process, from identifying user needs through to presentation for manufacturing.

parts.content:
**Figure 1**: A photo of a cyclist using a prototype smart helmet with LED lights on in traffic.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/3e5df926-ebab-4284-829f-f11d28b723fa)

Identify one reason the designers would benefit from observing real users in urban cycling environments.
parts.markscheme: - To identify visibility and safety challenges cyclists face in real traffic conditions. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 2**: Photographs showing two different physical prototypes: one made with cardboard and duct tape, and one with 3D-printed components and basic electronics.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/e780e66f-1845-4584-8636-2b25d3d2c1c1)

Outline two ways that developing low-fidelity prototypes (**Figure 2**) benefits the early stages of the design process.
parts.markscheme:
- They are inexpensive and quick to produce, allowing rapid iteration. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They help gather early feedback from users without committing to final materials. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe key considerations in transitioning from low-fidelity to high-fidelity prototyping, using examples from **Figure 2**.
parts.markscheme:
- Designers must ensure the prototype increasingly reflects functional performance, such as integrating actual electronic components (e.g. working LEDs) instead of visual indicators. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Materials should be selected that better represent the final product (e.g. 3D-printed plastic vs. cardboard). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The design must be refined to test real-world usability, such as fit, comfort, or durability during cycling. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 3**: A slide from a product presentation showing renderings of the helmet in 2 colour options, and key features listed


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/9a7dd885-6a16-43fb-9557-9757fbcfc588)

Explain how **Figure 3** supports both internal stakeholders (engineers/designers) and external stakeholders (investors/users) in decision-making.
parts.markscheme:
- The visual presentation helps designers clarify feature integration (e.g. LED placement). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Engineers can use the renderings and feature information to inform feasibility and manufacturing decisions (e.g. housing/assembly considerations). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Investors can understand market appeal through the shown colour options and the stated features/benefits. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- End users are more likely to understand how the product solves their problems through clear visuals and feature descriptions. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss how modelling and testing with real users can be used to evaluate and refine the design of the smart helmet.
parts.markscheme:
- Testing identifies pain points, such as difficulty activating turn signals while riding. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- User feedback on comfort, such as helmet fit and pressure points, informs ergonomic refinement. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Visibility tests (e.g. LED brightness in daylight) ensure the product meets safety requirements. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Real-world usage reveals technical flaws like false crash detection triggers. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Feedback on voice navigation clarity helps adjust audio positioning. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Iterative testing ensures the final product meets user expectations and usability standards. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the effectiveness of using digital product presentations (as seen in **Figure 3**) during the final stage of the design process.
parts.markscheme:
- Digital slides help convey complex information clearly through visuals. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They improve stakeholder engagement by highlighting features and benefits concisely. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Multiple views or colour options help assess final design decisions. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Rendered presentations simulate real-world use for client reassurance. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- However, digital images may not fully represent tactile feedback or mechanical limitations. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Overreliance on visuals might hide unresolved technical issues not visible in renderings. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: What does the design process represent in the context of design thinking?

options.content: A way to analyse market trends
A model consisting of five sections

questions.specification: What is involved in the ideation and modelling stage?

options.content: Creating one solution to a problem
Generating multiple approaches to solve the problem

questions.specification:
What key principle does the double diamond design model represent?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/885246f3-8259-4802-a3fc-8d4ec463a12e.jpeg)

options.markscheme: This refers to cost-benefit analysis or material selection, not the structural thinking process of the double diamond.

options.content: Using user feedback to sketch logos
options.markscheme: While user feedback is part of the process, sketching logos is a specific task and does not define the overarching principle of the model.

options.content: Divergent and convergent thinking throughout the process
options.markscheme: The double diamond model visually represents the stages of opening up (divergent thinking) to explore possibilities and closing down (convergent thinking) to focus on specific solutions.
This refers to organizational management rather than the design thinking process.

questions.specification: Which of the following describes primary research?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B. Design in practice > B2.1 The design process > B2.1.2 Research in the design process
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B2.1.2 Research in the design process
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for the definition of primary research, which is a core part of the 'Research' phase in the design process, where designers collect first-hand data.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A. Design in theory > A2.1 User-centred research methods > A2.1.4 User-centred research methods
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A2.1.4 User-centred research methods
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Primary research is a fundamental component of user-centred research methods, which involves direct interaction or data collection from potential users.

options.markscheme: This uses existing data collected by others, so it is secondary research.
This uses information produced by another organisation, so it is secondary research.

options.content: Analyzing published user reviews from a third-party website
options.markscheme: This analyzes existing user feedback already published elsewhere, so it is secondary research.

options.content: Collecting first-hand data relevant to the context
options.markscheme: Primary research involves collecting original, first-hand data directly relevant to the design context (e.g., interviews, observations, surveys you conduct).

questions.specification:
Why would a designer create a visual like the one in the image?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/c3fa4439-d183-4e1d-8787-5270c6f68405.jpeg)

options.content: To identify steps and emotions in a user's experience to find design opportunities
options.markscheme: A user journey map is a visual representation of the process a person goes through to achieve a goal. It is used to understand user emotions and identify 'pain points' where design improvements can be made.

options.content: To showcase packaging options for presentation

questions.specification: When empathizing with users, what tool do designers use to represent a user group?
Why is using design specifications important in solution development?

options.content: They replace the need for research
They establish clear criteria to satisfy
They ensure only one solution is developed

questions.specification: How is product analysis used by designers?

options.content: To understand function and features of existing products

questions.specification: Which design thinking phase involves synthesizing research insights to define the user needs and frame a clear design challenge?

options.markscheme: Correct: The Define phase involves synthesizing research findings to identify user needs and frame a clear problem statement or design challenge.
Incorrect: The Ideate phase focuses on generating a broad range of potential solutions to the defined challenge.
Incorrect: The Prototype phase involves creating scaled-down or preliminary versions of the product to explore and test ideas.
Incorrect: The Evaluate phase (often referred to as 'Test' in Design Thinking) involves gathering feedback on the prototypes to refine the solution.

parts.content: Describe how a user journey map can support the design process.
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** pain points: It pinpoints areas of friction in the user experience that need to be solved through design interventions. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Visualize** interactions: It maps every touchpoint the user has with the product, providing a holistic view of the design challenge. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Facilitate** communication: It uses visual narratives like storyboards to align stakeholders and ensure a shared understanding of user needs. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Promote** user-centred ideation: It encourages designers to focus on user needs and emotions throughout the design process. <marks>1 mark</marks>


<note>Award [1] for each well-described contribution to the design process, up to [4 marks max].</note>

questions.specification: What activity involves mapping a user’s journey through observation?
What is the main goal of ongoing research in the design process?

options.content: To understand user needs and find real-world solutions

questions.specification:
What research method is being used in the image?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/fa5fb50e-fc12-46de-bb53-ca23778e8d65.jpeg)

options.markscheme: Secondary research involves using existing data that has already been collected by others. The image shows a researcher collecting new, original data directly from a subject.
Cost analysis is a financial process used to evaluate the expenses of a project, not a method for gathering consumer data as shown in the image.

Brand testing may be the specific application or objective of the research, but 'primary research' is the correct categorical classification for the method of first-hand data collection being illustrated.
Primary research (also known as field research) involves collecting new, first-hand data for a specific purpose, such as through the interview and observation shown in the image.

questions.specification:
The diagram shows a typical Design Cycle.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/cb0a9572-23f4-4398-b195-44b43ff31279.jpeg)

Why are design specifications important at the stage indicated by the arrow?

options.content: They replace the need for testing
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Design specifications do not replace testing; rather, they provide the quantitative and qualitative benchmarks against which the product is eventually tested and evaluated.

options.content: They define criteria that guide and assess the solution
options.markscheme: Correct. The design specification acts as a bridge between the research phase and the design phase. It provides a list of requirements that guide the designer during idea generation and serves as the criteria for assessing the success of the final solution.

options.content: They act as legal product disclaimers
options.markscheme: Incorrect. While specifications may include safety standards, their primary function in the design cycle is to guide development and evaluation, not to serve as legal disclaimers.
Incorrect. Design specifications are technical documents used by designers, clients, and manufacturers. They are not intended for marketing or advertising purposes.

questions.specification: A start-up design team is developing a modular desk organiser targeted at students who need compact, adaptable workspace storage. Their process includes early ideation sketches, low-fidelity cardboard mockups, and stakeholder presentations using digital renderings.

parts.content:
The team first began the design process with different sketches such as the following:


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/2f552ac4-8df7-45b5-b8a3-bd0cfa595e1f)

Identify one advantage of using hand-drawn sketches in the early stages of the design process.
parts.markscheme:
- Allows for quick expression of ideas without digital tools. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Any relevant benefit of sketching such as flexibility or speed. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
The team then moved on to creating cardboard models of the desk organiser. 

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/35e39463-9e30-4865-b648-df1af95b943b)

i.) Outline two reasons the team chose to build low-fidelity prototypes before finalising their design.
parts.markscheme:
- Helps visualise the size and proportions of components. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enables quick testing of configurations for stackability and access. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe one limitation of using cardboard for evaluating long-term usability of the desk organiser.
parts.markscheme:
- Cardboard lacks durability, so it cannot simulate long-term wear and tear. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Identifies a material-based shortcoming relevant to the design's function. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
The team then began creating a 3D render of the desk organiser. 

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/68c9265b-4e50-4b79-8e4e-01c00169de9d)

Explain how this type of presentation helps communicate the final concept to both users and manufacturers.
parts.markscheme:
- Helps users visualise how it fits into their environment, such as on a small desk. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provides manufacturers with accurate dimensions and material indications. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Clarifies how parts interact (e.g., drawer pulls, stackable modules). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Communicates aesthetic choices clearly through colours and finishes. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss how the design team could use user feedback during prototype testing to improve the final product.
parts.markscheme:
- Users may suggest additional compartments for small items (e.g. paperclips). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Feedback may identify issues like drawers being too shallow. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Students might prefer colour options that match their room aesthetics. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Comments on ease of moving/stacking units may lead to better grip features. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Observing user interaction could highlight design inefficiencies. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Feedback can inform branding decisions such as logo placement. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the effectiveness of using a modular design strategy in this product’s development.
parts.markscheme:
- Allows users to personalise their organiser based on specific storage needs. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increases product appeal by offering scalability (e.g., expand storage later). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces manufacturing waste by standardising repeated parts. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- May lead to increased complexity in ensuring structural stability when stacked. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Difficulties in keeping all modules aligned can frustrate users. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Adds value and flexibility, but may raise production cost. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the importance of modelling and prototyping throughout the design process.
parts.markscheme:
- Modelling allows for early visualisation and concept testing <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Prototypes test functional aspects under real conditions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Feedback from models leads to rapid refinement <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps evaluate size, ergonomics, and user interaction <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Can highlight design flaws or safety issues early <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Aids communication between stakeholders and clients <marks>1 mark</marks>
- High-fidelity models can simulate actual performance <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Informs decisions before investing in final production <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supports iterative testing to meet design specifications <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [1] for each clearly justified point about modelling or prototyping, up to [9 marks].</note>

questions.specification: A design team is developing a portable, collapsible electric kettle aimed at frequent travellers. The product must balance compactness, safety, and user-friendly features. The team proceeds through several key stages in the design process.

parts.content:
To begin, the team benchmarks their idea by comparing similar products currently on the market.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/af104e84-3365-4653-a874-7eca78b9fa22)

Identify one advantage of conducting a competitive product analysis at the start of a design process.
parts.markscheme: - Helps identify market gaps and opportunities for product differentiation <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
After market research, the team begins generating multiple ideas for the collapsible kettle.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/9c2f1182-0e0b-4c65-bc9a-1b713eec08dd)

Outline two reasons why sketching is important during the ideation phase.
parts.markscheme:
- Allows rapid exploration of multiple ideas <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Communicates ideas clearly among team members and stakeholders <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Identify one limitation of using sketches for evaluating product usability.
parts.markscheme: - Sketches lack physical interaction, so user comfort and handling can’t be tested <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
Once the design has been refined, a working prototype is produced for physical testing.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/61c49ece-9914-4cd7-844f-37eb6e97b6f4)

Explain how this high-fidelity prototype would support user testing.
parts.markscheme:
- Allows users to test heating time and safety features <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reveals issues with durability and waterproof sealing <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps evaluate ease of folding and storage in real scenarios <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supports validation of ergonomics and interaction with buttons or handles <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss how user feedback collected from field testing might shape the final kettle design.
parts.markscheme:
- Feedback may highlight unclear button labels that need redesign <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Users could suggest improvements to the fold/unfold motion <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reports of slow heating may prompt changes in coil placement <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Complaints about water spillage might drive lid redesign <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Insights help refine both function and user experience <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Repeated feedback themes guide prioritisation of design changes <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the value and drawbacks of using a design cycle model in this product’s development.
parts.markscheme:
- Provides a structured, repeatable approach to solving design problems <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ensures all key stages from research to evaluation are covered <marks>1 mark</marks>
- May slow progress due to formal stage-by-stage approach <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Can reduce flexibility or creativity if followed too rigidly <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encourages iteration, leading to a more user-focused product <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Might require more time or resources than ad hoc development <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
Reading market research is considered as:

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXc7RCFW67sY6eAWXfJMBtLdp3vDcEwxsv1WvqihZ49JIxgWEIyqy8g88gt95_03KiQlW3efdmACUyfAy__IMiZcdPUySczQ1rqfWVKWw6j_4S3jIoS3Bw7vNW5FPx7snGRjJTx9?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.markscheme: Trend mapping involves identifying patterns and future directions based on data, but the act of reading existing reports itself is secondary research.
Primary research refers to the collection of new, first-hand data for a specific purpose.
Secondary research (or desk research) involves the use and analysis of data that has already been collected by others, such as market research reports.
Prototyping is the development of a preliminary version of a product to test a concept or process.

questions.specification: What does user observation primarily aim to identify?
